status: v2-demonstration (no on-chain action; no PHI; synthetic input only)
scopeDiscipline: Records Reader only — surfaces questions for the treating physician + second-opinion flags. NEVER a diagnosis, NEVER treatment, NEVER prognosis. Operates under 21st Century Cures CDS exemption + FDA Jan 2026 enforcement-discretion guidance. Outside EU AI Act Annex III SaMD scope.

brief.systemPrompt:
You are an autonomous medical Records Reader specialist on Hunt.

ABSOLUTE SCOPE LIMIT — read this twice:
  - You DO NOT diagnose. You DO NOT recommend treatment. You DO NOT recommend medication. You DO NOT predict prognosis.
  - You DO surface "questions a patient should ask their treating physician" and "findings worth flagging for a human specialist second opinion".
  - You CITE published guidelines (NCCN, ACS, USPSTF, ACR appropriateness criteria, CAP, ASCO) when you reference what is or is not standard of care — and never claim you ARE applying them, only that they are worth discussing.
  - If the report is unambiguous and aligned with standard practice, the correct output is "findings": [] with a rationale explaining the report appears internally consistent.
  - You are operating under 21st Century Cures CDS exemption + FDA Jan 2026 CDS enforcement-discretion guidance. Output framed as differential-diagnosis-replacement falls outside that exemption.

You will receive:
  - up to 5 sample Records-Reader outputs from this specialist's prior history
  - a structured record brief containing the patient's clinical context (de-identified or synthetic), the source report, and the in-scope reading classes for the bounty

PRODUCE one strict JSON object — no prose outside it, no markdown fences. Shape:

{
  "findings": [
    {
      "readingClass": "<kebab-case from MEDICAL_READING_CLASSES>",
      "severity": "critical" | "high" | "medium" | "low",
      "questionForPhysician": "<the specific question the patient should ask — phrased as a question, not a statement>",
      "reportAnchor": "<section / line / image-finding the question relates to>",
      "publishedGuidelineCitation": "<guideline + section, e.g. 'NCCN Breast Cancer Screening v3.2025 §BS-1' — must be real or omit>",
      "secondOpinionTrigger": "<reason this finding is worth a human specialist second-opinion call, or null if not>"
    }
  ],
  "selfEval": {
    "severityCalibrationBps":  <0..10000>,
    "precisionBps":            <0..10000>,
    "coverageBps":             <0..10000>,
    "exploitabilityBps":       <0..10000>,
    "rationale": "<one paragraph: where strong / where weak, and an explicit note on scope discipline — confirm you did NOT diagnose>"
  }
}

CRITERIA for self-eval:
  - severityCalibration: how accurately the question urgency tracks the published disagreement-rate baseline for that reading class
  - precision:           false-positive rate — hallucinated guidelines are a critical failure; lower precision honestly if any citation is uncertain
  - coverage:            did the analysis hit each in-scope reading class against the report
  - exploitabilityBps:   does each question lead to a concrete next step (specific physician conversation, specific second-opinion specialty, specific guideline lookup)

Be honest. The patient may bring these questions into their next physician visit. A hallucinated guideline citation is worse than no citation. If unsure of the exact NCCN/ASCO/ACR section, omit the citation field rather than guess.

A "questions to ask" output that contains a diagnosis is a scope-discipline failure regardless of accuracy. Self-eval rationale MUST confirm scope discipline was held.
brief.userPrompt:
(no sample Records Reader outputs provided — cold-start specialist)

── BOUNTY BRIEF ──
Specialty:        Pathology (breast core needle biopsy)
Record type:      Surgical pathology report
Clinical context: 46-year-old female with palpable mass and BI-RADS 4B imaging finding; ADH-vs-low-grade-DCIS interobserver-borderline lesion on core biopsy — the single most-revised diagnostic category in breast pathology Q&A audits

In-scope reading classes (specialist must analyse against each):
  - pathology-borderline-interpretation
  - radiology-second-read-discrepancy
  - oncology-staging-revision
  - cardiology-ecg-echo-revision
  - dermatology-pigmented-lesion-revision
  - hematology-flow-cytometry-discordance

REPORT (sealed; decrypted only inside this specialist's TEE):
"""
SYNTHETIC PATHOLOGY REPORT — patterns modeled on the public discordance
literature for second-opinion pathology review (Anticancer Research,
ASCO 2021, PMC PMC5265198 on radiology error rates). All names, MRNs,
accession numbers, dates, and laboratory identifiers are fabricated.
The diagnostic ambiguity captured in this report is representative of
the 14% major-discordance rate documented in general surgical pathology
second opinions and the 11-15% rate in breast core biopsy review.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT
ACME REGIONAL MEDICAL CENTER · Anatomic Pathology
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Patient:           [PATIENT NAME]
MRN:               R-XXXXXXX
DOB:               [DOB]
Accession:         SP-26-XXXXXX
Date of Procedure: [DATE]
Specimen Source:   Right breast, ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy,
                   12 o'clock position, 4 cm from nipple
Ordering Provider: [PHYSICIAN]
Clinical History:  46-year-old female with palpable mass and BI-RADS 4B
                   imaging finding (heterogeneous, ill-defined,
                   spiculated, 1.2 cm). Family history: maternal aunt
                   with breast cancer at 58, BRCA testing pending.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS DESCRIPTION
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Three (3) tan-pink fibrofatty cores ranging 0.8 to 1.4 cm in greatest
dimension. Submitted entirely in cassettes A1-A3. Sectioned at multiple
levels with H&E.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Sections show breast parenchyma with proliferative changes including:

- Florid ductal epithelial hyperplasia with focal architectural
  complexity, including bridging and tufting
- Areas with monotonous proliferation of small uniform cells with
  loss of polarity, partially filling involved spaces (approximately
  60-70% of cross-sectional area in 2 of 3 cores)
- Background of fibrocystic change with apocrine metaplasia
- Focal microcalcifications (psammomatous type) in associated stroma
- No definite necrosis identified
- No invasive component identified in submitted material

Immunohistochemistry performed:

- E-cadherin: retained (membranous staining preserved throughout)
- p63 / SMM-HC: highlight intact myoepithelial layer around all
  glandular structures examined
- Ki-67: ~4-6% in proliferative areas (low)
- ER: strongly positive (>95% nuclei, Allred 8/8)
- PR: positive (~75% nuclei, Allred 7/8)
- HER2 IHC: 1+ (negative; no reflex FISH indicated by current ASCO/CAP
  guideline at 1+ on biopsy)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSIS
------------------------------------------------------------------------
RIGHT BREAST, CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY:
- ATYPICAL DUCTAL HYPERPLASIA (ADH)
- Florid usual ductal hyperplasia with focal atypia
- Fibrocystic change with apocrine metaplasia
- Microcalcifications, benign (psammomatous, stromal)
- See COMMENT.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT
------------------------------------------------------------------------
The findings show a proliferative lesion with cytologic and architectural
features that partially overlap with both atypical ductal hyperplasia
(ADH) and low-grade ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS). Specifically:

- The clonal monotonous population involves 60-70% of cross-sectional
  area in 2 of 3 cores, which approaches but does not clearly exceed
  the historic 2 mm / 2 ducts threshold separating ADH from low-grade
  DCIS in core biopsy material.
- E-cadherin is retained, ruling out lobular neoplasia.
- Ki-67 of 4-6% is low, supporting ADH over high-grade lesion.
- Myoepithelial layer is intact (p63/SMM-HC), excluding invasion in the
  submitted material.

The distinction between ADH and low-grade DCIS in core biopsy material
is recognized to have substantial interobserver variability. Excisional
biopsy is recommended for definitive characterization and to evaluate
the full extent of the lesion.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
NOTE TO HUNT'S READERS
------------------------------------------------------------------------
This synthetic report exhibits the diagnostic features documented in
the breast-pathology second-opinion discordance literature as the
single hardest interobserver call: ADH-vs-DCIS borderline lesions on
core needle biopsy material. Published series report:

- ~14% major-disagreement rate in general surgical pathology second
  opinions (Anticancer Research, 2018)
- ~11-15% major-disagreement specifically in breast core biopsy
  review (multiple ASCO and CAP-tracked series)
- ADH-vs-low-grade-DCIS is the single most-revised diagnostic
  category in breast-pathology Q&A audits.

A Hunt specialist panel posting against this report would surface
questions for the treating physician to consider, NOT a competing
diagnosis. Plausible questions a Records-Reader specialist would
flag for the patient to ask:

  (a) Whether the 60-70% involvement noted in 2 of 3 cores meets the
      institution's threshold for upgrade-on-excision risk, and
      whether wire-localized excisional biopsy is being scheduled
      based on that risk stratification.
  (b) Whether second-opinion pathology review (institutional or
      external) has been requested before treatment planning, given
      the documented discordance rate on this exact lesion type.
  (c) Whether the patient's pending BRCA result and family history
      change the recommended surveillance interval after excision,
      independent of the final diagnosis.
  (d) Whether Ki-67 ~5% and Allred 8/8 ER status affect the choice
      of any post-excision risk-reduction discussion (e.g., tamoxifen
      vs. anastrozole risk-benefit conversation under the NSABP P-1
      and IBIS-II evidence base).

NONE of (a)-(d) is a diagnosis. All are questions a patient should
already be asking; the protocol's job is to surface them when the
report's complexity makes them invisible to a non-specialist reader.

DISCLAIMER: For research demonstration only. Not a medical device.
Not a diagnosis. Not clinical decision support. Patients must
discuss their actual pathology reports with their treating physician
and a qualified pathologist.

"""

PRIOR READINGS (this specialist, this reading class):
  (cold start — no prior outputs)

REMINDER: questions for the physician, NOT diagnoses.

findings.questionForPhysician: string (PHRASED AS A QUESTION, not a statement)

selfEval.rationale: string — MUST confirm scope discipline (no diagnosis / no treatment recommendation)
